title: Portland City Council Work Session-Unbudgeted Housing Funds  3/05/26

chapters.summary: Councilor Smith: Can you hear us online, Councilor Smith? Yes, I am. We can hear you. Thank you.
chapters.headline: Councilor Smith: Can you hear us online, Councilor Smith
chapters.gist: Counselor Smith on gun control

chapters.summary: Council recently identified approximately $106 million in housing-related balances that were not previously budgeted for allocation. This session is designed primarily as a working discussion among council. First, we're going to focus on clarifying the available options and then on policy priorities.
chapters.headline: Portland City Council will hold a work session on housing funding on Wednesday
chapters.gist: Portland City Council Work Session

chapters.summary: Michael Bonacore, interim director for the Portland Housing Bureau. We'll be going through this, uh, presentation in 3 chunks: some background, um, some information about how we have managed our accrued funds. And then we'll get into the detail of the subfund balances.
chapters.headline: Michael Bonacore: Thank you for the time spent preparing this presentation
chapters.gist: Portland Housing Bureau Board Presentation

chapters.summary: An external financial auditor audits these financial statements every year and issues audit opinions. The ending fund balances for the prior fiscal year are displayed in the ACFR. Presenting funding in this way requires translation for Council and the public. That is not how PHB has ever presented its budget in the past.
chapters.headline: Portland Housing Bureau issues Annual Comprehensive Financial Reports annually to City Council
chapters.gist: ACFR and AFKAR

chapters.summary: $21 million in unspent rental services office funds is a specific issue. The remaining amount in the total of $106 million is largely committed. However, you all have the opportunity to make different choices than what PHB has proposed.
chapters.headline: $21 million in unspent rental services office funds is a specific issue
chapters.gist: The $106 Million to Spend

chapters.summary: We typically wait until we have at least $20 million to put out in a solicitation. It gives us the opportunity to define what kinds of goals we're trying to achieve with the money that we're putting out into the community. On the other side of the sunburst is the development process and the disbursement of funds.
chapters.headline: Next slide provides more background on the PHB's solicitation process
chapters.gist: The Proposed No-HUD Housing Funds

chapters.summary: Currently, there are about 40 projects at various development stages. And PHB staff are engaged in that process throughout the entire lifecycle. The next few years are not resource-rich in the way that they have in the last several years for us.
chapters.headline: Next slide looks back at last 5 years of housing production as funding declines
chapters.gist: PHB's role in housing production

chapters.summary: The fund balances are either committed or what the plans are for their future. In the committed uses column, you'll see specific projects that we have made commitments to. On the planned uses side, you see where we have plans for future solicitations. Those are policy trade-off conversations for you all to have.
chapters.headline: Next slide shows the fund balances and what the plans are for their future
chapters.gist: Housing Fund balances, committed uses and planned uses

chapters.summary: The intention is to do a preservation solicitation next year with these funds. Given the wind down of current TIF districts and the lag in revenue from new ones, these funds figure more prominently in our ability to fund preservation and/or new development. All of these funds have very prescribed uses for them.
chapters.headline: Next slide. Here's a little bit of a ragtag collection of funds
chapters.gist: The City's Property Funds

chapters.summary: Next slide. These are the TIF districts. On the left side, you see committed uses. Those are for specific projects and for our home repair program. Some of the districts have money that is not currently allocated to specific projects.
chapters.headline: Next slide shows committed uses of TIF funds for specific projects
chapters.gist: TIF districts and their uses

chapters.summary: The 6 tables that I just talked you through total about $93 million. There is an additional $8.9 million of RSO funds under council consideration. If you subtract out the TIF and the Inclusionary Housing Fund line items, that totals to $112 million. We are now happy to take your questions and conversation.
chapters.headline: The 6 tables that I just talked you through total about $93 million
chapters.gist: Discussion of the RSO Funding

chapters.summary: Councilor: I had a question about the housing beginning fund balances. From that information provided, the total beginning fund balance across all housing funds, general fund not included, is $145 million. But right now we've been having a discussion of $106 million, and I'm wondering if you can explain the discrepancy.
chapters.headline: Councilor asks question about housing beginning fund balances
chapters.gist: City Budget Office: Housing Beginning Fund Balance

chapters.summary: How often does your office coordinate with the accounting manager to get us accurate numbers? I want to be really careful in continuing the narrative that there are inaccurate numbers. This is the work session for us to discuss it as a body.
chapters.headline: How often does your office coordinate with the accounting manager to get us accurate numbers
chapters.gist: How often does the city Controller coordinate with the Accounting Manager to get

chapters.summary: One of my priorities is to see how much we can get to the general fund. My good friend Adrian Delgado is in the audience, so I'll ask Adrian to come up and give us some guidance. Um, they're not going to necessarily match up to what was presented, but hopefully we can figure that out.
chapters.headline: One of my priorities is to see how much we can get to the general fund
chapters.gist: City Attorney's Office Provides Guidance on the General Fund

chapters.summary: Councilor Smith's proposed amendment has $5.4 million back to the general fund from the short-term rental 2% category. So I'm just wondering how many opportunities are there for new things where the general Fund gets money.
chapters.headline: Councilor Smith's proposed amendment has $5.4 million going to general fund
chapters.gist: Local Housing Trust Fund and Short-term Rentals

chapters.summary: My understanding is the Bureau was planning to use up all of that money through a preservation solicitation. Does that mean things like doing mortgage buydowns or other things to stabilize the existing pool of affordable housing? We would not necessarily expect the state to step up and pay a share of those projects.
chapters.headline: It looks like Bureau planned to use up all of that money through a preservation solicitation
chapters.gist: Bureau of Affordable Housing: Spending $106 Million on existing housing

chapters.summary: What is the maximum subsidy per unit rule? With the housing bonds for new production, there was a max award of $150,000 per unit, and for preservation projects, $100,000. Are there any concerns about the legal challenges to any of the ways that Smith One would spend the $20.7 million?
chapters.headline: You talked about the maximum subsidy per unit rule for housing bonds
chapters.gist: Minimum subsidy per unit for short-term rentals

chapters.summary: Councilor Clark: It would just be helpful to know the number of units that correspond with these investments. After the loan closeout, it seems like we should have some sort of tracking or follow-up or accountability. If you could follow up with that, I'd really appreciate it.
chapters.headline: Councilor Avalos: I appreciate getting a better understanding of these investments
chapters.gist: Councilor Avalos on the Investment Funds

chapters.summary: There's $7 million available in the South Park box for acquisition, new construction, or preservation. I've heard in the rumor mill that that's money that may be going to PSU for their cultural performing arts plan. How does that divide up between new and preservation? What's the split?
chapters.headline: Councilor Green has a question on the TIF on the South Park Blocks
chapters.gist: The TIF on the South Park Blocks

chapters.summary: There's only about $55.9 million of money at Council's discretion with degrees of discretion. Of that, $20.7 million is very much at council's discretion. There's no other planned uses for it. It doesn't jeopardize existing NOFAs, just future planned solicitations.
chapters.headline: There's only about $55.9 million of money at Council's discretion
chapters.gist: Portland Housing Board: $55.9 million of money at Council

chapters.summary: There's a sum of $4.8 million that has, to my knowledge, no project identified. Council could direct the Bureau to more discreetly direct those dollars in that TIF district. This would be again towards housing dollars and not necessarily ECTAV.
chapters.headline: Councilor, you've included a number of the TIF balances
chapters.gist: Portland TIF balances, Amended Action Plan

chapters.summary: There's a line item that says $18.9 million RSO-Under Council Consideration. Plus $1.8 million from FALTAO. Can you please help me understand that? It's just you're crosswalking it to the fall TAO action, correct, for us?
chapters.headline: Councilor Kunal has some technical questions regarding proposed RSOs
chapters.gist: RSO-Under Council Consideration

chapters.summary: Is it correct for me to interpret this as saying the first dollar in the door could take 7 to 17 years to result in a physical building. Has anyone done a comparative analysis with other jurisdictions on that figure?
chapters.headline: The first dollar in the door could take 7 to 17 years to complete
chapters.gist: On the 7- to 17-Year Construction Time

chapters.summary: Construction excise tax can be used for affordable housing. 15% goes back to to the state. 4% is administrative fee retained by permitting for the city. 50% is transferred to Housing Bureau to fund finance-based incentive programs. That adds up to 104.
chapters.headline: Construction excise tax can be used for affordable housing, but what uses restricted
chapters.gist: Construction Excise Tax

chapters.summary: The $11.1 million has to remain within the 0 to 60-day first line here. The $8.6 million short-term rental fee has to be related to the collection. The River District funds are completely separate from other uses.
chapters.headline: Councilor Novick asked about STR, short-term rental funding
chapters.gist: Councilor Novick on Short-Term Rentals Funding

chapters.summary: The largest reductions are in federal grants, TIF, and then bonds. Can you speak to the bonds part of it? And just give a little more insight into how that came to be projected this way?
chapters.headline: The last question is about projected decline in revenue from federal grants and bonds
chapters.gist: Portland tax revenue forecasted decline, including federal grants, bonds

chapters.summary: Michael: Were those funds reported to Moss Adams or Baker Tilly? Did they know about those funds? When we were supposed to see those dollars, we were not made aware of them. The auditor didn't see the full authorization, and that's a problem.
chapters.headline: Councilor: Were those funds put in our audit, Councilor
chapters.gist: Approval of $106 Million in Funds

chapters.summary: Councilor Novick: "We can't begin to plan for a NOFA that's happening in the future if you're the only ones who know about the money" Oregon budget law hasn't changed. Now when we go back for this year, our auditors are going to have to look at it and our audit is going to be wrong.
chapters.headline: Michael Novick: It was hidden from the council and the mayor
chapters.gist: Councilor Novick on NoFA Controversy

chapters.summary: Michael: Are there any other monies in these pots that you have not told us about that we should know about above the $106 million? Jonas: There's, I think, some gaps in information about the $137,000. Councilwoman: Is that a nuance of the way accounting rules around the ACFR are developed?
chapters.headline: Is there any other monies in these pots that you have not told us about
chapters.gist: Are there any other monies in these pots that we should know

chapters.summary: Jonas: There are clearly still gaps, but you started to explain to us why some of those gaps exist. I'd like to see that on paper, on these charts that we have here, as soon as possible. For better or worse, at this point in the conversation, we have a lot of interested stakeholders.
chapters.headline: Jonas, I really appreciate the answer you just gave on the $137 million
chapters.gist: Councilors on the Budget and Spending

chapters.summary: Why are these funds set aside to use in 2028? When we know we have a housing crisis now? Can you explain the thinking at the Bureau in waiting an additional 2 years to even talk about those funds?
chapters.headline: Bureau has $30.4 million in uncommitted funds
chapters.gist: The $30.4 Million in Uncommitted Funds

chapters.summary: There's 35% for affordable production and preservation of 60% AMI or less. Are those restrictions in our code, or are they in an agreement with the state? Are there any other restrictions around how we spend the funds?
chapters.headline: There's 35% for affordable production and preservation of 60% AMI
chapters.gist: Construction Excise Tax: Affordable Housing

chapters.summary: Could preservation of affordable housing also mean things that would fill units that are currently unfilled in affordable housing complexes? I'm wondering if preservation dollars as defined here could be used in those cases or not.
chapters.headline: Could preservation of affordable housing also mean things that fill unfilled units
chapters.gist: Does preservation of affordable housing mean anything for the city?

chapters.summary: Councilor Ryan: What is the annual operational budget for Portland Housing Bureau? The entire bureau, not each program, the total that is needed since it's not legally available from restricted accounts. Councilor Ryan wants to know the total number of dollars that could be eligible to be transferred to the general fund going into the '26-'27 budget.
chapters.headline: Councilor Ryan asks what is the annual operational budget for Portland Housing Bureau
chapters.gist: Portland Housing Bureau General Fund Controversy

chapters.summary: There's clarity that the $20.7 million in RSO funds needs to be allocated by council. A decision must be made about whether proposed uses, particularly in CET and short-term rental, will be allocated. Strategic questions start to get us into the next step, and I'll stop.
chapters.headline: Technical question number 2: Going forward, we can accept previous allocated amounts
chapters.gist: Technical Question #4

chapters.summary: Councilor Koyama Lane or Council President Dempsey have anything they want to ask. Why don't we aim to go until about 11:05 if that's okay? Just get this last round of technical questions and then we'll take a break.
chapters.headline: Councilor Novick asks last technical question before taking a break
chapters.gist: Council Takes a Break on Technical Questions

chapters.summary: Council is considering choosing between rent assistance, preservation of existing affordable housing, building new housing, and the general fund. Could you give us some thoughts as to how we would go about evaluating the relative bang for the buck?
chapters.headline: Council is considering choosing between rent assistance, preservation of existing affordable housing
chapters.gist: Choosing between rent assistance and construction

chapters.summary: The balances in the ACFR are accurate as audited by the external auditor. If there's something wrong, if we have it in the wrong bucket, let us know and we will change it. We know we're in a housing crisis. I'm really pressing and urging my colleagues to get this on the streets really quickly.
chapters.headline: Michael Smith: The balances in the ACFR are accurate as audited
chapters.gist: Councilor Ilana Pirtle-Ginney on the

chapters.summary: Thank you to our panel of experts here. We are going to take a quick recess. Please be back at 11:18, and we'll begin the second half of our discussion. Do you have any coaches lingering out there?
chapters.headline: We are going to take a quick recess. Let everybody get settled
chapters.gist: President Trump on the Russia Investigation

chapters.summary: Council has had substantial conversations about housing investments. Today's conversation is meant to build on that work. I'm going to walk through a few discussion prompts one at a time. Feel free to use your time to speak on any question if that feels appropriate.
chapters.headline: Council has had substantial conversations about housing investments over the past months
chapters.gist: Discussion Prompts

chapters.summary: Council wants to hear your understanding of our current options or constraints currently before Council. Are there categories or constraints that you believe should be clarified before we move further into discussion allocation? We're going to do about 10-ish minutes each question.
chapters.headline: Council members will ask questions on allocation of city funds
chapters.gist: Discussion of the Fiscal Framework

chapters.summary: Councilor: I want to think about approaching this discussion in terms of two tiers of council action. Tier 1 is immediate requirement to act now on the $20.7 million. Second tier is more of a call for strategic programming.
chapters.headline: Council members will discuss housing funding in a special session on Tuesday
chapters.gist: Council discussion of the housing crisis

chapters.summary: In Tier 1, I'd really like to see us think about dividing the $20.7 million as follows. Could help 450 to 4,500 households stay stabilized. $1.9 million for eviction defense. $8.8 million in what I think is actually a supply-side solution.
chapters.headline: We've talked about it in two different ways here, which gets to the stabilization question
chapters.gist: Tier 1: Unallocated Housing Set-aside Funds

chapters.summary: Councilor Novick: I think we have a generational opportunity with this one-time money, to maybe do some pilot social housing acquisition. Council President Dempsey: I like the two-tier approach, but would not envision returning any dollars to the general fund.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green: I think we should talk about what our priorities are
chapters.gist: Council President Dempsey on the Two-Tier Housing Plan

chapters.summary: One preliminary question I have is All of the various proposed rent assistance categories, is that short-term rent assistance? How many people does that— do those pots envision giving people rent assistance for how many years?
chapters.headline: All of the various proposed rent assistance categories are short-term
chapters.gist: Is Short-Term Rent Assistance appropriate?

chapters.summary: Councilman: I would like us to have an extensive discussion about rent, temporary rent assistance versus what Councillor Green is focused on. He says we get more bang for our buck by preserving and enhancing the use of existing affordable housing. Councilman says he'll vote no on anything that only allocates $20 million.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green wants to focus on temporary rent assistance versus stabilizing existing affordable housing
chapters.gist: County Council: Stabilizing existing affordable housing funding

chapters.summary: I am wondering about the $4.8 million because the first that I had heard about us using those dollars was today. I don't think it is the right use or best use for these dollars. I hope that we focus as much as we can on things that have long-term effects.
chapters.headline: Councilor Avalos asks about potential uses of $4.8 million
chapters.gist: The $4.8 Million Controversy

chapters.summary: Mayor Wilson: This afternoon, you're going to be presented with the spring TAL and the budget deficit that we have right now today. The first recommendation that I, I'd like you to consider is taking a portion of these funds. Second, and only my second request would be to also focus on rent assistance for those currently experiencing homelessness.
chapters.headline: Mayor Wilson makes some recommendations regarding homelessness and Broadway Corridor funding
chapters.gist: Mayor Wilson makes three recommendations to reduce service impacts and homelessness

chapters.summary: Councilor Green: The ones that have specific projects are better left alone in this moment. Looking at the 58.1 as already committed is something I'm comfortable doing. The part that I'm interested in is already in the 30.4, I believe.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green: I agree on scope of dollars that we're looking at
chapters.gist: Council Discusses the CET Project

chapters.summary: Councilor Koyama-Lane: Use of short-term rental dollars for in-house economic development on the tourism side. Councilor Murillo: Big supporter of rent mortgage buydowns, social housing acquisition. Green: $4.8 million going to Broadway Corridor given TIF River District restriction.
chapters.headline: First, the thing that I think has been missing is activating our spaces through tourism
chapters.gist: Portland's Short-Term Rentation Plan

chapters.summary: I'm hearing folks talk about using the funds to lower rents going forward so you can do some sort of restructuring of debt. And then I've also heard it being talked about as for some of the affordable housing providers that are kind of underwater. So if someone wants to clarify, that would be helpful.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green: I'm hearing different explanations of rent buydown
chapters.gist: Rent Buydown and Bridge of Assistance

chapters.summary: Next 2 are to talk about these 2 particular buckets around rental eviction prevention, all of that, and development projects. Which of these programs would you prioritize retaining at their current level? And are there new ideas or approaches related to housing development that councilors would like to consider?
chapters.headline: Next 2 questions are about rental eviction prevention and development projects
chapters.gist: Rental Services, Development Projects

chapters.summary: Councilor Avalos: We need to test these trade-offs here. Rent assistance on the one hand and, and these other kind of more maybe perhaps long-term sorts of interventions. Councilor Corral-Malane: I was inspired by the rent buydown presentation. We could have a prioritization schedule.
chapters.headline: Councilor Novik raises question about balancing rent assistance and other long-term interventions
chapters.gist: Rent Assistance and the NoFA

chapters.summary: Councilor Pedagani: I worry that if we do that, people might think of that as a ceiling rather than a floor. Public dollars in the housing market should only be used to fill spaces where the private market won't meet. Council President: Is there an opportunity for the city to have an ongoing ownership stake in housing?
chapters.headline: Councilor Pedagani expresses concern over proposed $30 million housing fund
chapters.gist: Council President on the $20 Million Housing Proposal

chapters.summary: Councilor Smith: I think we need to spend some of those dollars on rent assistance. Also believe the 2% money that we have the $8.6 million in, in my proposal, it gives $5 million back to the general fund. Clark: I'd love to start seeing you in the queue.
chapters.headline: Councilor Smith says we need to spend some of those dollars on rent assistance
chapters.gist: Councilor Smith on the 2% Funding

chapters.summary: With assets, just like the assets we currently have in our public works, they require ongoing maintenance and ongoing money. The last thing that I'm concerned about, and we'll talk about this later, is the realignment process for the city.
chapters.headline: With assets, they require ongoing maintenance and ongoing money
chapters.gist: Council Member on Property Ownership

chapters.summary: If we do the rent assistance, it's going to take until after July 1st. One of the proposals that I had put forward was to identify community-based organizations like the Sun School groups who do rental assistance. I think for potentially for something like a new program, like an eviction legal defense, that will take longer.
chapters.headline: If we do rental assistance, it's going to take until after July 1st
chapters.gist: Portland Housing Board: Rental Assistance and Eviction Legal Defense

chapters.summary: Council President: We have to incentivize middle-income units to be built in parts of Portland. Broadway Corridor, an extension of the Pearl District, will be the next, I hope, mixed income, densely populated, mixed building income, he says.
chapters.headline: Council President says creating more units is his number one priority
chapters.gist: Portland housing: Affordable, mixed-income units

chapters.summary: There are, there are definitely a lot of unfunded, unsupported supports and expenses that the providers are bearing themselves. Why has the choice been not to take any rent on those vacancies instead of just taking less than you had hoped to take?
chapters.headline: Multnomah County has a number of subsidized housing units with vacancies
chapters.gist: Building expenses have gone up in the Portland area

chapters.summary: I want to talk about a systemic solution to rental buydown. Shelter services are fine if the participants are seen, served, and assisted to permanent housing. But the vacancy rates due to slow turnaround is unacceptable. And the other, safety. Let's just do a better job taking care of the affordable housing.
chapters.headline: Councilor Ryan wants to talk about affordable housing issues in Portland
chapters.gist: Portland Housing Committee: Rental Buydown

chapters.summary: Councilor Khamelain agrees with Councilor Dunphy on ownership ownership stake. We need to move away from studios and one-bedrooms and start really forcing the conditions of our money being to three and four-bedroom units. And finally, the assessment of what Councilor Green was saying about shocks and emergent conditions changing our approach to some of our prior pencilings.
chapters.headline: I agree with Councilor Khamelain on eviction representation being core priority
chapters.gist: Council: Issues discussed on preservation, eviction representation

chapters.summary: Portland Street Response is not a homelessness response program. It is a behavioral health health, public safety, first responder. vacancies are a huge part of the problem. They're much cheaper to fill a vacancy than it is to build a new place. I think we put up a vacancy, a vacant property fee.
chapters.headline: Councilor Perdogani: Portland Street Response should not be repurposed
chapters.gist: Portland Street Response: Vacancies are the enemy of homelessness

chapters.summary: There's some really important projects on there. The Urban Plaza funding will help us leverage a necessary conversation. Two projects involve homeownership opportunities for low-income families. I think it is absolutely critical that if we decide to fund specific projects, those stay on the books for what we're looking at.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green raises questions about money that may not be spent on specific projects
chapters.gist: Proposal to spend $51.4 million on housing

chapters.summary: I agree 100% that mixed-income neighborhoods are the model. I just don't think AMI is the way to do it. Councillors Green and Murillo promised me that they will produce an actual social housing banner. I'm looking forward to seeing the size and the color scheme.
chapters.headline: Councilor Zimmerman agrees 100% that mixed-income neighborhoods are the model
chapters.gist: Portland Housing Committee Discusses Mixed-In Neighborhood Housing

chapters.summary: Mayor: I agree with a lot of what has been said. I just want to put a finer point on the conversation around eviction prevention and rental assistance. It sounds like people want a braided, blended approach to these dollars. Council will discuss how we take what we learned today and move forward.
chapters.headline: Council members share their thoughts on eviction prevention and rental assistance
chapters.gist: Work Session on Eviction Prevention and Rental Assistance
